paragraphs.text: In my contribution to the consultation on the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act, I would like to highlight one aspect that has received rather little attention in the policy debate so far: the impact of agentic AI on EU consumer law. Agentic AI is widely seen as the next major evolution in the field of artificial intelligence. Unlike chatbots, AI agents are able to browse the web and autonomously perform multi-step tasks on behalf of users. The rise of agentic AI could have a transformational impact on the functioning of digital markets and change the way how consumers interact with digital services. This technological development coincides with a policy debate about the future of EU consumer law that could lead to the adoption of a Digital Fairness Act. Considering the rapid developments in the field of agentic AI and its potentially transformative impact on digital markets, one would expect that AI agents take centre stage in the policy debate about the future of EU consumer law. However, this is not the case. It rather seems that agentic AI is the blind spot in the policy debate leading to the Digital Fairness Act. There is a significant risk that the upcoming reform of consumer law will ignore one of the key developments in digital markets. As a consequence, the Digital Fairness Act could be outdated even before it enters into force. Against this background, this paper argues that the Digital Fairness Act should regulate agentic AI. Updating the existing regulatory framework for agentic commerce is of key importance for two reasons: It is necessary for enabling innovative agent-based business models and for protecting consumer autonomy in tomorrows agentic economy. In this sense, ensuring that EU consumer law is agent-ready is essential for creating the legal infrastructure for the future of the Digital Single Market.
